items.q: Did Prohibition stop Americans drinking alcohol?
items.correct: No — drinking continued in illegal speakeasies supplied by bootleggers, and organised crime figures such as Al Capone grew rich on the trade.
items.misconception: Prohibition successfully eliminated drinking in America until it was repealed in 1933.
items.random_wrong: Prohibition only banned drinking in public parks.
items.why: Students assume a law in force was a law obeyed; enforcement was weak and demand stayed high, which is why Prohibition is studied as a failed policy.

items.correct: The Wall Street Crash exposed deeper weaknesses — overproduction, unequal wealth, weak banks and shrinking demand — which combined to produce a prolonged slump.
items.misconception: The 1929 Crash alone instantly caused the whole Depression.
items.random_wrong: The Depression was caused by American spending on the Second World War.
items.why: Students compress cause into a single event; examiners reward seeing the Crash as a trigger acting on long-term structural weaknesses.

items.q: Did the New Deal end the Great Depression?
items.correct: It cut unemployment, restored confidence in banks and provided relief, but recovery was incomplete — full employment only returned with rearmament and the Second World War.
items.misconception: The New Deal completely ended the Depression within Roosevelt's first term.
items.random_wrong: The New Deal abolished the US dollar to fight the Depression.
items.why: Students overstate the New Deal's success; unemployment remained high through the late 1930s, a standard evaluation point.

items.q: Which constitutional amendment introduced Prohibition in 1920?
items.why: Correct answer: The 18th Amendment. “The 19th Amendment” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: A shanty town built by homeless people during the Depression, named mockingly after President Hoover
items.misconception: A New Deal agency that built dams
items.random_wrong: An illegal bar selling alcohol during Prohibition
items.why: It's A shanty town built by homeless people during the Depression, named mockingly after President Hoover. “A New Deal agency that built dams” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which president launched the New Deal in 1933?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Franklin D. Roosevelt. “Herbert Hoover” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why did many American farmers struggle during the 1920s boom?
items.correct: Overproduction and falling crop prices left many farmers in debt while demand declined
items.misconception: Prohibition made it illegal to grow grain
items.random_wrong: The New Deal taxed farms heavily
items.why: Correct answer: Overproduction and falling crop prices left many farmers in debt while demand declined. “Prohibition made it illegal to grow grain” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Wall Street Crash occur?
items.why: The real figure is 1929 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
